concepts.title: Hydrolysis of Salts
concepts.conceptBody:
HYDROLYSIS OF SALTS

Dissolution of different salts in water does not always result in neutral solutions. For example, aqueous solution of copper sulphate is acidic whereas aqueous solution of sodium acetate is basic and aqueous solution of sodium chloride forms neutral solution. This is due to the dissociation of the salt in water to form ions. This process of the reaction of anion or cation of the salt with water to produce an acidic or an alkaline solution is called hydrolysis. Thus, hydrolysis is reverse of neutralization.

The equilibrium constant of such a reaction is called hydrolysis constant.

Hydrolysis of anion $\left( {{\text{A}}^{-}} \right)$

$\underset{\text{ Salt }}{\mathop{\text{BA}}}\,+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\to \underset{\text{ Acid }}{\mathop{\text{HA}}}\,+\underset{\text{ Base }}{\mathop{\text{BOH}}}\,$

or ${{\text{A}}^{-}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\to \text{HA}+\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$

$$

Hydrolysis of cation \[({{B}^{+}})\]

$$

The fraction of the total salt that gets hydrolysed at equilibrium is called degree of hydrolysis. It is written as ‘$\text{h}$’.

concepts.title: Salt of weak acid and strong base
concepts.conceptBody:
(1) Salt of weak acid and strong base

Here, the anion $\left( {{\text{A}}^{-}} \right)$is a stronger base than $\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$, hence it undergoes hydrolysis to give free $\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$ ions. Therefore the resulting solution will be basic in character having $\text{pH}$ greater than 7. For example.

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COONa}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}+\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}+\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$

Weak acid

Other examples of this of salts are $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COONa},\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}},\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{3}}\text{P}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$, etc.

The aqueous solution of a salt of weak acid and strong base it alkaline. For the general reaction:

$\underset{\text{ Salt }}{\mathop{\text{BA}}}\,+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons \underset{\begin{smallmatrix}

\text{ Strong } \\

\text{ base }

\end{smallmatrix}}{\mathop{\text{BOH}}}\,+\underset{\begin{matrix}

\begin{smallmatrix}

\text{ Weak} \\

\,\,\,\text{acid }

\end{smallmatrix} \\

{} \\

\end{matrix}}{\mathop{\text{HA}}}\,$

or ${{\text{B}}^{+}}+{{\text{A}}^{-}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{B}}^{+}}+\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}+\text{HA}$

or ${{\text{A}}^{-}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons \text{HA}+\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$

This is called anion hydrolysis .

Hydrolysis constant

The hydrolysis constant may be written as:

${{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}=\frac{[\text{HA}]\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]}{\left[ {{\text{A}}^{-}} \right]}$ … (i)

For weak acid $\text{HA},\text{HA}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}^{+}}+{{\text{A}}^{-}}$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}=\frac{\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]\left[ {{\text{A}}^{-}} \right]}{[\text{HA}]}$ … (ii)

Also, ${{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}=\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]$ … (iii)

Multiplying equation (i) and (ii) and dividing by equation (iii), we get

$\frac{{{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}\cdot {{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}}{{{\text{K}}_{\text{W}}}}=\frac{[\text{HA}]\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]}{\left[ {{\text{A}}^{-}} \right]}\times \frac{\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]\left[ {{\text{A}}^{-}} \right]}{[\text{HA}]}\times \frac{1}{\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]}=1$

$$

The hydrolysis constant ‘ ${{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}$’, of the salt is inversely proportional to the dissociation constant,
‘${{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}$’ of the weak acid. Therefore, the weaker the acid, the greater is the hydrolysis constant of the salt.

concepts.title: Degree of Hydrolysis
concepts.conceptBody:
Degree of hydrolysis

If the original concentration of the salt in the solution is ‘$c$’ mol/litre and ‘$h$’ is the degree of hydrolysis at that concentration, then

${{\text{A}}^{-}}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}+\text{HA}$

Initial concentration

c

0

0

Concentration of equilibrium

c(1 – h)

ch

ch

The hydrolysis constant, ${{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}=\frac{[\text{HA}]\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]}{\left[ {{\text{A}}^{-}} \right]}$

$=\frac{\text{ch}\cdot \text{ch}}{\text{c}(1-\text{h})}=\frac{\text{c}{{\text{h}}^{2}}}{(1-\text{h})}$

If ‘$h$’ is very small as compared to 1, we can assume $1-h\approx 1$.

\[{{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}=\text{c}{{\text{h}}^{2}}\]

or \[\text{h}=\sqrt{\frac{{{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}}{\text{c}}}\]

Substituting the value of ${{K}_{h}},h=\sqrt{\frac{{{K}_{W}}}{{{K}_{a}}\cdot c}}$

$\text{pH}$ of the hydrolysed salt solution $\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]=\text{CH}$

$\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]=\frac{{{\text{K}}_{W}}}{\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]}=\frac{{{\text{K}}_{W}}}{\text{ch}}$

Substituting the value of h,$\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]=\frac{{{\text{K}}_{\text{W}}}}{\text{c}}\sqrt{\frac{{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}\cdot \text{c}}{{{\text{K}}_{\text{W}}}}}$

or $$

Now, $\text{pH}=-\log \left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]$

$=-\log {{\left( \frac{{{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}\cdot {{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}}{\text{c}} \right)}^{\frac{1}{2}}}$

$\text{pH}=\frac{1}{2}\left[ \log {{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}-\log {{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}-\log \text{c} \right]$

Now, $-\log {{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}=\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}-\log {{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}=\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}$

$$

concepts.title: Salt of Strong acid and weak base
concepts.conceptBody:
(2) Salt of a Strong Acid and Weak Base

Let us take a salt, $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{Cl}$. It is a salt made from the reaction of strong acid, $\text{HCl}$ and weak base, $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{OH}$.

\[\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{Cl}(\text{s})\xrightarrow[{}]{{}}\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{Cl}(\text{aq})\xrightarrow[{}]{{}}\text{NH}_{4}^{+}(\text{aq})+\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}(\text{aq})\]

Out of $\text{NH}_{4}^{+}$and $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$, only $\text{NH}_{4}^{+}$ion hydrolyses and let its degree of hydrolysis be $\text{h}$ and ${{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}$ be its hydrolysis constant. Let ‘$\text{c}$’ $\text{M}$ be the concentration of the salt, so the concentration of $\text{NH}_{4}^{+}$ would also be ‘c’ M.

$\text{NH}_{4}^{+}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{OH}+{{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}$

Initial conc. c 0 0

Conc. at equilib $\text{c}(1-\text{h})$ ch $\operatorname{ch}$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{eq}}}=\frac{\left[ \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{OH} \right]\left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]}{{{\left[ {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right]}^{2}}\left[ \text{NH}_{4}^{+} \right]}$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{eq}}}\,\,\,{{\left[ {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right]}^{2}}=\frac{\left[ \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{OH} \right]\left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]}{\left[ \text{NH}_{4}^{+} \right]}={{K}_{h}}$ … (i)

Multiplying numerator and denominator of equation (i) by \[O{{H}^{-}}\] gives

$\frac{\left[ \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{OH} \right]\left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]}{\left[ \text{NH}_{4}^{+} \right]\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]}={{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}$

$\therefore $ $$ $\text{(since }\left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]={{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}\text{ and }\frac{\left[ \text{NH}_{4}^{+} \right]\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]}{\left[ \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{OH} \right]}={{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}\text{)}$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}=\frac{{{\text{K}}_{\text{W}}}}{{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}}=\frac{\text{ch}\times \text{ch}}{\text{c}(1-\text{h})}=\frac{\text{c}{{\text{h}}^{2}}}{1-\text{h}}$

Assuming $\text{h}$ to be smaller than 0.1, it can be ignored with respect to 1 . So, $1-\text{h}\approx 1$.

${{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}\approx \text{c}{{\text{h}}^{2}}$

$\text{h}=\sqrt{\frac{{{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}}{\text{c}}}=\sqrt{\frac{{{\text{K}}_{\text{W}}}}{{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}\times \text{c}}}$

$\therefore \quad \left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]=\text{ch}=\text{c}\times \sqrt{\frac{{{\text{K}}_{\text{W}}}}{{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}\times \text{c}}}=\sqrt{\frac{{{\text{K}}_{\text{W}}}\times \text{c}}{{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}}}$

Taking $\log $ of both the sides

$\log \left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]=1/2\log {{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}+1/2\log \text{c}-1/2\log {{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}$

Multiplying both sides by –1,

$-\log \left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]=-1/2\log {{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}-1/2\log \text{c}+1/2\log {{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}$

$\therefore $ $\text{pH}=1/2\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}-1/2\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}-1/2\log \text{c}=1/2\left( \text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}-\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}-\log \text{c} \right)$

$\left( \text{since },-\log {{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}=\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}\text{ and }\log {{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}=-\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}} \right)$

$\therefore $ $\text{pH}=7-1/2\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}-1/2\log \text{c}.$

Since, two terms are to be subtracted from 7, so, the $\text{pH}$ of such salt solutions would be less than 7 and will behave acidic in nature. ‘$c$’ in the above expression represents concentration of the ion undergoing hydrolysis and not the concentration of the salt.

concepts.title: Salt of weak acid and weak base
concepts.conceptBody:
(3) Salt of Weak Acid and a Weak Base

In this case both the cation and anion undergo hydrolysis to the same or different extents. The resulting solution may be neutral, acidic or basic depending upon the relative strengths of acids and bases. The hydrolysis may be written as:

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COON}{{\text{H}}_{4}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons \underset{\text{ weak acid }}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}}}\,+\underset{\text{ weak base }}{\mathop{\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{OH}}}\,$

Some common examples are $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COON}{{\text{H}}_{4}},{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}} \right)}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}},\text{AlP}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$, etc.

The aqueous solution of a salt of strong acid and weak base is acidic. For the general reaction:

\[\underset{\text{ Salt }}{\mathop{\text{BA}}}\,+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons \underset{\text{Weak base}}{\mathop{\text{BOH}}}\,+\underset{\text{ Weak }}{\mathop{\text{HA}}}\,\]

or ${{\text{B}}^{+}}+{{\text{A}}^{-}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons \text{BOH}+\text{HA}$

This involves anionic and cationic hydrolysis.

Hydrolysis constant

The hydrolysis constant may be written as:

${{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}=\frac{[\text{BOH}][\text{HA}]}{\left[ {{\text{B}}^{+}} \right]\left[ {{\text{A}}^{-}} \right]}$ … (vi)

For weak acid, $\text{HA}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}^{+}}+{{\text{A}}^{-}}$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}=\frac{\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]\left[ {{\text{A}}^{-}} \right]}{[\text{HA}]}$

For weak base $\text{BOH}$

$\text{BOH}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{B}}^{+}}+\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}=\frac{\left[ {{\text{B}}^{+}} \right]\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]}{[\text{BOH}]}$

Also, ${{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}=\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]$

Multiplying equation (vi) by equation $\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]$, we get

${{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}=\frac{[\text{BOH}][\text{HA}]\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]}{\left[ {{\text{B}}^{+}} \right]\left[ {{\text{A}}^{-}} \right]\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]}$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}=\frac{[\text{BOH}][\text{HA}]\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]}{\left[ {{\text{B}}^{+}} \right]\left[ {{\text{A}}^{-}} \right]\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]}$

$=\frac{[\text{BOH}]}{\left[ {{\text{B}}^{+}} \right]\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]}\cdot \frac{[\text{HA}]}{\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]\left[ {{\text{A}}^{-}} \right]}\cdot \left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]$

$$

Degree of hydrolysis

If the original concentration of the salt in the solution is ‘ $\text{c}$ ‘ mol/litre and ‘ $h$ ‘ is the degree of hydrolysis at that concentration, then

${{\text{B}}^{+}}+{{\text{A}}^{-}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons \text{BOH}+\text{HA}$

Initial concentration

c

c

0

0

Concentration at equilibrium

$\pm c(1-h)$

$\text{c}(1-\text{h})$

ch

h

The hydrolysis constant, ${{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}=\frac{\text{ch}.\text{ch}.}{\text{c}(1-\text{h})\times \text{c}(1-\text{h})}=\frac{{{\text{h}}^{2}}}{{{(1-\text{h})}^{2}}}$

If ‘$\text{h}$’ is very small as compared to 1, we can assume $1-\text{h}\approx 1$.

${{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}={{\text{h}}^{2}}$

or $\text{h}=\sqrt{{{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}}$

Substituting the value of $h=\sqrt{\frac{{{K}_{w}}}{{{K}_{a}}\cdot {{K}_{b}}}}$

Here the degree of hydrolysis is independent of the concentration of the solution. The weaker the acid and the base, the greater is the degree of hydrolysis of the salt.

$\text{pH}$ of the hydrolysed salt solution

${{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}=\frac{\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]\left[ {{\text{A}}^{-}} \right]}{[\text{HA}]}$

$\left[ {{H}^{+}} \right]={{K}_{a}}=\frac{[HA]}{\left[ {{A}^{-}} \right]}$

$\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]=\frac{{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}\cdot \text{ch}}{\text{c}(1-\text{h})}=\frac{{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}\cdot \text{h}}{(1-\text{h})}$

$\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]={{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}\cdot \text{h}$

$={{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}\sqrt{{{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}}$

Substituting the value of ‘ $h$ ‘, $\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]={{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}\sqrt{{{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}}$

or $\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]={{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}\sqrt{\frac{{{\text{K}}_{\text{W}}}}{{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}\cdot {{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}}}$

$=\sqrt{\frac{{{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}}{{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}}}$

Now, $\text{pH}=-\log \left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]$

$=-\log {{\left( \frac{{{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}\cdot {{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}}{{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}} \right)}^{\frac{1}{2}}}$

$\text{pH}=-\frac{1}{2}\log {{\text{K}}_{\text{W}}}-\frac{1}{2}\log {{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}+\frac{1}{2}\log {{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}$

$$

Knowing the molar concentration ‘$\text{c}$’ of the solution, ${{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}},{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}$ and ${{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}$, the $\text{pH}$ of the solution can be calculated. It is clear from the above equation that $\text{pH}$ of the solution will depend upon the $\text{pK}$ value of the acid and the base.

If $\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}<\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}},\text{pH}$ of the solution will be less than $1/\text{pK}{{\text{w}}_{\text{w}}}$ and consequently the solution will be acidic.

If $\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}>\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}$, then $\text{pH}$ of the solution will be more than $1/2\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}$ and hence the solution will be alkaline.

If $\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}=\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}},\text{pH}$ of the solution will be equal to $1/\text{pK}{{\text{w}}_{\text{w}}}$ and hence the solution will be neutral.

concepts.title: Salt of Strong acid and Strong base
concepts.conceptBody:
(4) Salt of A Strong Acid and a Strong Base

Let us consider a salt, $\text{NaCl}$ made by the reaction of strong acid, $\text{HCl}$ and strong base, $\text{NaOH}$.

$\text{NaCl}(\text{s})\xrightarrow[{}]{{}}\text{NaCl}(\text{aq})\xrightarrow[{}]{{}}\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}(\text{aq})+\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}(\text{aq})$

Neither of the two ions of the salt undergoes hydrolysis. So, the solution contain only the equilibrium of ionization of water.

$2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}(\text{l})\rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}(\text{aq})+\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}(\text{aq})$

Thus, the $\text{pH}$ of such salts always remain constant and the solution behaves neutral at $\text{pH}=7$ at $25\text{C}$.

concepts.title: Common ion effect
concepts.conceptBody:
COMMON ION EFFECT

The addition of an ionic salt having a common ion (anion or cation) to weak acids or weak bases, suppresses their degree of dissociation (following Lechatlier’s principle).

Weak Acids:

Let $\text{HA}$ be a weak acid (like $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH},\text{HCN}$ etc.) & ${{\text{B}}^{+}}{{\text{A}}^{-}}$be the ionic salt containing common anion $\left( {{\text{A}}^{-}} \right)$that is added to the acid.

$\text{H}{{\text{A}}_{\text{aq}}}\rightleftharpoons \text{H}_{\text{aq}}^{+}+\text{A}_{\text{aq}}^{-}$

$\text{B}{{\text{A}}_{\text{aq}}}\rightleftharpoons \text{B}_{\text{aq}}^{+}+\text{A}_{\text{aq}}^{-}$

Now, the solution has excess of ${{\text{A}}^{-}}$ions. This means increasing concentration of products, in an equilibrium state. (Following Lechatlier’s principle) the reaction ($\text{HA}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}^{+}}+{{\text{A}}^{-}}$) must go in backward direction, in order to mullity the effect of added ${{\text{A}}^{-}}$ions. As a consequence, amount of ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$in new equilibrium state will be less than before, or one can see that the degree of dissociation of acid (HA) is decreased.

Quantitative Aspect:

$\text{HA}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}^{+}}+{{\text{A}}^{-}};$its degree of dissociation $=\alpha $ is given by:

$\alpha =\sqrt{\frac{{{\mathbf{K}}_{\text{a}}}}{\text{c}}}$

Let M molar BA be added to it \[\alpha '\]be its new degree of dissociation

\[\begin{matrix}

BA & \rightleftharpoons & B_{aq}^{+} & + & A_{aq}^{-} \\

{} & {} & M & {} & M \\

\end{matrix}\]

(Assuming BA to be 100% ionised)

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}}

\text{HA} & \rightleftharpoons & {{\text{H}}^{+}} & + & {{\text{A}}^{-}} \\

\text{c}-\text{c}{{\alpha }^{\prime }} & {} & \text{c}{{\alpha }^{\prime }} & {} & \text{c}{{\alpha }^{\prime }} \\

\end{array}$

Now in solution:

$[\text{HA}]=\text{c}-\text{c}{{\alpha }^{\prime }}$ ; $\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]=\text{c}{{\alpha }^{\prime }}$

$\left[ {{\text{A}}^{-}} \right]={{\left[ {{\text{A}}^{-}} \right]}_{\text{HA}}}+{{\left[ {{\text{A}}^{-}} \right]}_{\text{BA}}}=\text{c}{{\alpha }^{\prime }}+\text{M}$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}=\frac{\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]\left[ {{\text{A}}^{-}} \right]}{[\text{HA}]}=\frac{\left( \text{c}{{\alpha }^{\prime }} \right)\left( \text{c}{{\alpha }^{\prime }}+\text{M} \right)}{\left( \text{c}-\text{c}{{\text{a}}^{\prime }} \right)}=\frac{{{\alpha }^{\prime }}\left( \text{c}{{\alpha }^{\prime }}+\text{M} \right)}{1-{{\alpha }^{\prime }}}$

$\Rightarrow $ ${{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}=\text{c}{{\alpha }^{\prime 2}}+\text{M}{{\alpha }^{\prime }}\left( \text{ as }1-{{\alpha }^{\prime }}\approx 1 \right)$

$\Rightarrow $ ${{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}=\text{M}{{\alpha }^{\prime }}$

(neglecting \[C\alpha {{'}^{2}}\] in comparison to $\text{M}{{\alpha }^{\prime }}$ as ${{\alpha }^{\prime }}\ll 1\,\,\And \text{ }{{\alpha }^{2}}\ll <1$]

$\Rightarrow \quad {{\alpha }^{\prime }}=\frac{{{K}_{\text{a}}}}{\text{E}}$

Note: One similar lines you can find ${{\alpha }^{\prime }}$ for a weak base, $\text{BOH}$ and adding ${{\text{B}}^{+}}$ions to it.
